The clause says the hiddenness did not become apparent to anyone — verb (negated past) comes before the theme of revelation.
segments.s546-g227: تهی‌دست را is the definite object marked with را ('the poor / the empty‑handed' as the beneficiary/target). مایه دادی is a light-noun + past-tense verb construction ('gave resources/substance'), with بسی after the verb as an adverb of quantity ('plenty/abundantly'). Verb (دادی) in past 2nd person concludes the clause.
segments.s546-g228: بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him/them'; شاد کردی is a causative result phrase ('you made (someone) happy'); دل هرکسی is the object phrase 'the heart of every person' (دل as the emotional object, هرکسی 'everybody'). Persian order here places the indirect object before the verb and the direct object (دل هرکسی) after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s546-g229: همان کودکان را is the definite/direct object ('those same children'). به فرهنگیان is a dative phrase ('to the cultured / to the men of learning'); Persian uses به + plural animates to mark the recipients. The object marker را fixes the children as what was given/entrusted.
segments.s546-g230: سپردی is the past 2nd-person verb 'you entrusted/handed over'. چو here means 'when', بودی = 'you were/it was' (past auxiliary sense) and together چو بودی ورا هنگ آن = 'when that time came / when you were at that moment'; ورا is a pronominal object 'to them/it' referring back to the children, and هنگ آن is a temporal phrase 'that moment/time.' The clause places the verb before the temporal phrase for poetic word-order.
segments.s546-g231: به هر برزنی is a locative/dative phrase ('in/to every town/village'); در دبستان specifies 'in a school'. The final بدی is an archaic past-tense verb meaning 'you gave/you established/you put' (verb at the end is normal in Persian poetry). Together the segment says you established/assigned a school in each town. (Note: بدی is an archaic poetic past form used here as 'you put/you gave'.)
segments.s546-g232: همان جای آتش‌پرستان names the location: 'the very place of the fire‑worshippers' (همان = 'the same/that very', جای = 'place', آتش‌پرستان = 'fire‑worshippers'). The closing بدی repeats the archaic past verb ('you set/placed it there'), so the full sense is that the schools were placed in the same sites formerly used by the fire‑worshippers.
segments.s546-g57: 'بدو' is the dative/adverbial 'to him' placed before the predicate for emphasis; 'باشد' is an optative/subjunctive/correlative copula here ('may it be' / 'let it be'); 'آباد' is the predicate adjective 'prosperous/inhabited' and follows the copula in this poetic order; 'شهر و سپاه' (city and army) are the compound theme/subjects which the optative refers to.
segments.s546-g58: 'همان' is the demonstrative 'the same/that very'; 'زیردستان' is a plural noun 'subordinates/vassals' in apposition; 'فریادخواه' is a deverbal noun/adjective 'one who cries for help' (literally 'cry‑seeker') and here functions as a predicate or epithet — the copula is omitted and understood ('are' / 'are those who').
segments.s546-g59: 'دبیران' (secretaries) is the plural subject; 'چو' is the simile marker 'like/as'; 'پیوند جان' is a genitive compound 'the bond of (my) life' (پیوند = bond/link, جان = life/soul); 'منند' is the older/poetic form of 'مانند' meaning 'are like' and serves as the copula at the end of the clause — the clause therefore literally reads 'secretaries like bond of life are'.
segments.s546-g60: 'همه' quantifies 'all'; 'پادشا' (پادشاه) is 'kings' (subject); 'بر' here functions adverbially/prepositionally ('with regard to, upon, in respect of') connecting to 'نهان' (hidden/secret); 'نهان' is an adjective 'hidden/secret'; 'منند' again is the copula 'are like' placed at the end — the clause says roughly 'all kings are, with respect to the hidden, alike' (poetic, elliptical word order).
segments.s546-g61: 'چو' here is temporal 'when'; 'رفتی' is 2nd‑person past 'you went'; 'سوی کشور' is a prepositional phrase 'toward the country/province'; 'کاردار' is an apposition or title 'kardār' (chargé d'affaires/governor) identifying the person referred to — the phrase is a temporal clause 'when you went to the province as chargé d'affaires'.
segments.s546-g62: The order 'بدو شاه گفتی' places the indirect object 'بدو' ('to him') before the subject 'شاه' ('the king') and the verb 'گفتی' (poetic/past 'said'); this inversion is common in epic poetry. 'درم خوار دار' is an imperative/instructional phrase: 'درم' (dirham, coin) + adjective 'خوار' ('low/cheap/despised') + verb 'دار' (imperative 'keep/treat'), literally 'keep/treat the dirham as cheap.' Note: some lexical items are archaic/poetic forms here.
segments.s546-g63: 'نباید' is an impersonal modal 'must not/should not'; 'که' introduces the content clause 'that'; 'مردم فروشی' is a nominalized verbal phrase 'selling of the people' (مردم = people, فروشی = sale/selling); 'به گنج' is a prepositional phrase 'for treasure' (literally 'to/for treasure') — together: 'It is not right that one sell the people for treasure.'
segments.s546-g64: 'که' here introduces the feared/result clause 'lest/so that'; 'برکس' should be read as the old/conjoined form 'بر کس' meaning 'for any person' (negative scope 'for anyone'); 'نماند' is the negative-form of 'ماندن' 'remain/be left' ('not remain'); 'سرای سپنج' is a noun phrase 'the house/dwelling of sponge' — a poetic epithet for a very humble dwelling — so the clause means 'so that no person's humble house will remain.'
segments.s546-g49: 'کسی را که' is a relative/object phrase: 'کسی' (someone) marked by the object marker 'را', followed by the relative 'که' ('who/that'). 'کمتر' here is comparative/adverbial ('poorer/less'), modifying the following noun phrase 'بدی خط و ویر' — literally 'badness of hand and ruin' (two nouns joined by 'و' that describe qualities). In older/poetic Persian such noun pairs form a descriptive bundle (here: poor handwriting and ruin).
segments.s546-g50: 'نرفتی' is the 2nd person singular past negative of 'رفتن' ('you did not go'); in classical usage 'رفتن' with a preceding 'کسی را' can function like 'to send/take someone' (transitive sense). 'به دیوان' = 'to the divan/royal office' (به = toward/into; دیوان = royal court/office). 'شاه اردشیر' is the title plus name ('King Ardashir').
segments.s546-g51: 'سوی کارداران شدندی به کار' is a compact clause: 'سوی' = 'to/toward', 'کارداران' = 'the managers/administrators', and 'شدندی' is the 3rd-person plural past 'they became / were made'. 'به کار' ('to the work/affair') indicates the sphere of their appointment — 'made managers of the affairs.'
segments.s546-g52: 'قلم‌زن بماندی بر شهریار' contains an archaic/polite 2nd-person past 'بماندی' ('you remained'); 'قلم‌زن' is the occupational noun 'penman/scribe'. 'بر شهریار' (بر + title) means 'in the presence of / for the king' — here 'you remained as the king's scribe.' Note the switch from plural 'they became' to addressing 'you' who stayed behind as scribe.
segments.s546-g53: 'شناسنده بد شهریار اردشیر' is a nominal group: 'شناسنده' literally 'knower/identifier' used here in the sense of an informer or recognizer, modified by the adjective 'بد' ('bad/ill-disposed'). 'شهریار اردشیر' is in apposition (the informer belongs to / works for King Ardashir). This is a compact epithet: 'Ardashir's ill-disposed informer.'
segments.s546-g54: 'چو دیدی' = 'when you saw' (چو = 'when'; دیدی = 2nd-person past of 'to see'). 'به درگاه' = 'at/to the court' ('به' = toward/into; 'درگاه' = royal court/threshold). 'مرد دبیر' = 'the man who is a dabir/scribe' (مرد = person/man; دبیر = scribe/secretary). The whole clause means 'when (he/you) saw the scribe at the court.'
segments.s546-g55: 'نویسنده گفتی که گنج آگنید' addresses or reports speech: 'نویسنده' = 'the scribe' (vocative/subject); 'گفتی' here functions as 'you said' in context (classical 2nd-person past), followed by the content clause marker 'که' ('that'). 'گنج آگنید' = 'the treasury was filled' (گنج = treasure/treasury; آگنید = 'filled with' / 'was filled' — a passive/participial sense). So: 'The scribe said that the treasury had been filled.'
segments.s546-g56: 'هم از رای او رنج بپراگنید' continues the report: 'هم' = 'also'; 'از رای او' = 'because of / from his counsel' ('رای' = counsel/plan; 'او' = his). 'رنج' = suffering/hardship. 'بپراگنید' is a classical verbal form meaning 'spread' (here intransitive/impersonal or 3rd-person plural past: 'suffering spread'). Together: 'and by his counsel suffering also spread.'
segments.s546-g65: همه here is a determiner meaning 'all' and heads a noun phrase that coordinates two concepts: راستی جوی is a compound idea 'truth‑seeking' (راستی 'truth' + جوی 'seeking'), and فرزانگی is a single noun 'wisdom'. The phrase functions as a wished/blessed possession (implicit: 'be yours') and so is left without an explicit verb.
segments.s546-g66: ز تو دور باد is an optative clause: ز 'from' (poetic), تو 'you', دور 'far', and باد is the classical optative particle 'may it be'. Together they mean 'may X be far from you'; آز و دیوانگی are the items governed by that wish ('greed and madness'). Word order puts the 'from you' phrase before the optative verb, typical in classical Persian.
segments.s546-g67: ز پیوند و خویشان preposes the source 'from bonds and relatives' (ز = 'from'). مبر is the negative imperative (do not take/recruit), and هیچ‌کس is the object 'no one'. The construction literally says 'From kin and ties do not take anyone' (do not recruit from your relatives).
segments.s546-g68: سپاه is the noun 'army/host' and آنچ is a relative/demonstrative 'that which' (i.e. 'the army that...'). The clause that follows (من یار دادمت) contains دادمت 'I gave you' with enclitic object -ت, and یار here means 'ally/companion' (the sense is that friends/forces were given). بس 'enough' closes the sentence. Word order is somewhat compressed: 'The host that I gave you (as ally/support) is sufficient.'
segments.s546-g69: درم is the amount 'a dirham' (direct object). بخش here functions as the imperative 'give' (classical بخش from بخشیدن). هر ماه is the adverbial frequency 'every month', and درویش را is the recipient with را used in classical Persian to mark the person receiving the gift. Word order places object/amount before the verb for emphasis: 'a dirham, give every month to the dervish.'
segments.s546-g70: مده is the negative imperative 'do not give'. چیز functions as 'anything/any thing' (object). مرد بداندیش را is the person‑phrase 'the ill‑intentioned man' marked by را (object/topic marker): 'do not give anything to a malicious/ill‑minded man.' Note the negative imperative + object order (مده چیز) which is common in older Persian.
segments.s546-g71: This is the protasis of a conditional: اگر 'if', کشور آباد 'the country prosperous' (noun + adjective), داری is the second‑person present 'you hold/you have' (i.e. 'you keep/maintain'), and به داد is an adverbial phrase 'by/with justice' (به + داد 'justice'). The clause thus means 'If you keep the realm prosperous by justice...'.
segments.s546-g72: بمانی is the apodosis verb 'you will remain' / subjunctive 'may you remain' (second‑person form of ماندن) and is followed by the subject pronoun تو (postverbal for emphasis) and the adjective آباد 'prosperous'. وز is an archaic/prepositional-like particle here meaning 'from/because of' (classical variant of از), linking داد 'justice' to شاد 'happy' — 'and by/through justice you will be glad.' Word order places the verb before the pronoun for classical poetic effect.
segments.s546-g249: Noun phrase + locative + verb: خراج (tribute/tax) is fronted, followed by the locative phrase اندر آن بوم (in that district), and the finite verb برداشتی sits at the end as a 2nd‑person past ('you took/levied'). اندر is the archaic/preclassical preposition 'in'.
segments.s546-g250: الترتیب (noun + adjective + verb): زمین کسان = 'the people of the land' (literally 'land‑people'), with the postpositive adjective خوار ('despised') modifying کسان. نگذاشتی is the 2nd‑person past negative of گذاشتن meaning 'you did not leave/allow', so the clause means 'you did not leave the land's people despised' (object pronoun is implied).
segments.s546-g251: Conditional clause introduced by گر ('if'). ایدونک is an adverbial particle meaning roughly 'now' / 'henceforth'; دهقان is the subject 'the peasant'. بدی here is used as a predicate indicating 'harm/badness' (stative) and تنگ‌دست is the adjective 'poor' — Persian omits the copula, so the sequence describes the peasant's state ('if now the peasant is harmed and poor').
segments.s546-g252: Result clause: سوی نیستی ('toward non‑existence') is a directional/figurative phrase, followed by گشته, the perfect/resultant verbal form 'has become/turned'. کارش ('his work/means' with enclitic ش = 'his') is the subject whose state has changed; ز هست ('from being') contrasts former existence with the present. Overall: 'his livelihood has become turned from being toward nonexistence.'
segments.s546-g253: Simple clause with verb + source phrase + coordinated objects: بدادی is a past tense verb 'you gave' (пoetic/persian past form), ز گنج is a source phrase 'from the treasury', and آلت و چارپای are the coordinated direct objects 'implements (tools) and four‑footed beasts (beasts of burden)'. Word order places the verb before or with its complements in this poetic line.
segments.s546-g254: Negated verb نماندی ('you did not leave / nothing remained') plus a relative/explanatory clause introduced by که. پایش ('its/his foot') + برفتی ('had gone/left') + ز جای ('from its place') form the subordinate clause. Together the line reads idiomatically as 'there remained nothing whose foot had not left its place' (i.e. nothing left standing/untouched).
segments.s546-g255: Imperative / vocative construction: ز دانا سخن = 'the speech/words from the wise (man)'. بشنو‌ای is the imperative 'listen!' with the vocative clitic ای attached; شهریار is the vocative 'O king/ruler'. The verb 'listen' addresses the ruler to heed wise counsel.
segments.s546-g256: Accusative/topic + adverbial phrase + verbal imperative: جهان را marks 'the world' as the definite object/topic; برین گونه = 'in this manner' (بر + این/ین + گونه = 'on/ in this way'); آباد دار is a causative/imperative sequence meaning 'keep/make prosperous' (آباد = prosperous, دار here forms the verbal phrase 'cause to be / keep'). The overall command: 'Make/keep the world prosperous in this way.'
